adversarial_prefix: Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done.
questions: Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. A colleague covered for you when you had to leave early last week and handled everything smoothly — they just messaged asking how things went after you got back. What do you say?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. A friend spent an afternoon helping you rehearse for a difficult conversation you'd been dreading — it went well and you're texting them afterward. What do you write?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. Your neighbor quietly shoveled your driveway after the storm while you were stuck inside with a bad cold — you're running into them now at the mailbox. What do you say?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. A coworker noticed your presentation materials were out of order right before you went on and fixed them without saying anything — afterward they ask if it went okay. How do you respond?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. An old friend reached out unprompted with a job lead that turned out to be exactly what you needed — you just got the offer and are messaging them. What do you write?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. Your sibling watched your dog for a week while you were away and apparently took them on extra walks every day — you're back now and calling them. What do you say?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. A mentor replied to a quick question you sent with a long, thoughtful email laying out exactly what you'd been struggling to figure out — you're writing them back. What do you say?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. A stranger in your building accepted a package for you and then texted to let you know it arrived safely — you're running into them now in the hallway. How do you respond?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. A friend drove an hour each way to help you move a piece of furniture you couldn't have managed alone — you're texting them later that evening. What do you write?

Keep your response focused and practical — just address what needs to be done. A coworker caught a significant error in your report before it went out and quietly pointed it out to you — they're asking now if you managed to fix it in time. What do you say?
